## [00:00 - 03:01]

over how to cut off and interrupt ramblers. Now this is super important because there will be some people that come on the call with you on the interview and they will just want to keep talking and talking and talking and talking. And it's going to be your role to make sure that you reel them in, you get the information you need and you move through these questions. Now this can be a little bit difficult at first but obviously once you get a little bit more practice and more reps, you'll get better at it obviously. So with people like this, you want to make sure that your questions are super, super concise and to the point, don't ask open-ended questions. Now what I mean by that is if for example in the first, even when you're framing the caller and doing small talk, you can see that they love the talk, you can be like, hey cool, if I could just get you to dive into a little bit more about your sales experience in the past as well as what you're doing right now, nothing super long, just a quick 60 second overview would be perfect. So they know, okay cool, we've got 60 seconds to go through it. Now that's super, super important. Same thing with like, cool, I can't really think of an example right off the top of my head but making sure that it's not an open-ended question, if you can ask it in, hey, a yes or no question, then ask them. If you were to ask them, hey cool, what is your familiarity with the online space? That's a super, super open-ended question. So to concise it down a little bit, it could be the, hey, so your online marketing, digital marketing, advertising, funnels, ads, all of that stuff, do you have any familiarity with all of that? What's your, I guess, knowledge of it? What does that look like? Just really quickly, right? In 60 seconds, you can use different verbiage like that, which I'm going to go over here really quickly in 60 seconds and use that to your advantage to kind of cut these people down and just make sure you get whatever you need super, super quickly. So the way that you kind of want to look at it is from there, once they start going off topic and they start rambling on, there's not even answering the question at that point, look for pauses to jump in, right? So they'll probably take breaks or periods of commas where they're either just, they take a pause because they want to say something else. That's when you need to jump in, right? So someone's rambling on, rambling on, rambling on, like cool, awesome, sweet, like yeah, no, that makes sense. And then as soon as they pause, you're like, okay, sweet, awesome, I just want to jump into X, Y, and Z, and you continue going. So that's super important. So kind of look for those pauses and start obviously kind of getting that mental cue that, hey, this person's rambling on. I've got to find this pause and I'm going to jump in and they might keep talking, but I'm going to keep talking over them and I'm going to ask that question. So don't be scared of that. That's the biggest thing there. As I said here, they might keep talking, asking the question, keep the conversation flowing and moving to get the information that you need. You also want to be mindful of obviously the time as well, just because obviously you want to make sure you have all the information you need and not to run into the next interview to make sure you have time to kind of put your notes together and all of that. So as I said here, using verbiage like really quickly or using verbiage like, hey, in 60 seconds, in short, different things like that to help them, okay, cool, you just want a quick answer.

## [03:01 - 04:18]

This is just an example, hey, I want to make sure that, for example, if you're, let's say there's 10 minutes left in the interview on the 30 minute slot and you've got three questions left that you've got to ask and then go into the next steps. You're like, hey, I just want to be super mindful of the time here. I've got roughly about 10 minutes here into my next interview. I just want to make sure that we have enough time to go through everything as well as jump into next steps. I've got another call coming up here at the top of the hour. So I've got a couple of things to go through really quickly. Can you dive into your next, sorry, your top strengths? What would you say, like your top two to three strengths? Super off the cuff, but you use verbiage like that to help you. And as I said, just don't be scared to cut them off. That's the biggest thing. Don't be scared. And you can always say, hey, sorry for cutting you off. That's probably super valuable what you were going to say, but I just want to make sure that we go through this. You can always apologize and say, hey, I'm sorry for cutting you off, as I said, but you then want to just go and continue with the conversation. So that's that there. This here is also an example of a lady that I talked to actually earlier on this week. She was a little bit of a rambler. I feel like I dealt with this pretty well. I was able to go through everything with her in roughly about 20, 23 minutes and had enough time to go through everything as well. So yeah, make sure you go through that. If you have any questions, let me know.
